title: Finding the ground state of spin Hamiltonians with reinforcement learning
data_sources: Spin Glass Server (University of Cologne, branch-and-cut exact solver)
core_conclusion: A PPO-based RL agent (COOL framework) learns dynamic temperature schedules for simulated annealing that surpass standard heuristics on two classes of spin Hamiltonians, generalizing across an entire class of nearest-neighbour spin glasses and achieving almost two orders of magnitude better scaling with system size than classic linear annealing, without any explicit knowledge of the Hamiltonian and while remaining effective in a destructive-observation mode mimicking quantum measurement constraints.
why_it_matters: Automates the heuristic black art of SA temperature scheduling; demonstrates class-level generalization to theoretically infinite spin glass instances from one training run; achieves polynomial vs. near-exponential scaling; bridges classical and quantum optimization via destructive-observation experiments; fully open and reproducible via Zenodo.
why_nature: Machine learning solving a fundamental NP-hard physics problem at the intelligence-science frontier NMI targets; COOL is a transferable reusable framework beyond condensed matter; quantum-inspired destructive-observation experiments address NMI quantum AI coverage; value-function phase-plane visualization provides genuine mechanistic interpretability.
added_value_to_my_research: Cast facility-location (EV chargers, clinics) as spin Hamiltonians solvable by COOL, replacing Monte Carlo/greedy heuristics with RL policies that scale polynomially with candidate site count
Replace convolutional encoder with GNN for irregular road-network graphs, enabling physics-informed graph RL for urban resource allocation
Model privacy-constrained mobile-phone retrieval as destructive measurement, designing RL-based sampling strategies within fixed privacy budgets for spatiotemporal density estimation
Warm-start COOL with free-energy machine priors to accelerate convergence on hard spatial optimization instances, encoding population-density constraints as Hamiltonian soft terms
Adopt value-function phase-plane visualization as XAI tool for spatial RL policies, projecting agent value onto accessibility-score vs. spatial-entropy axes for policy communication to planners

related.title: Spatial planning of urban communities via deep reinforcement learning
related.relation: applies deep RL to spatial facility placement, the urban analog of the spin-glass optimization solved in this paper

related.title: Efficient and scalable reinforcement learning for large-scale network control
related.relation: scalable RL policy for large graph-structured control, extending COOL's architecture toward irregular network topologies

related.title: Many-body control with reinforcement learning and tensor networks
related.relation: RL for quantum many-body control, directly extending the quantum-inspired destructive-observation paradigm in this paper

related.title: Free-energy machine for combinatorial optimization
related.relation: physics-based free-energy machine for combinatorial optimization, complementary method for warm-starting COOL on hard spin glass instances

related.title: Integrating statistical physics and machine learning for combinatorial optimization
related.relation: statistical physics and ML integration for combinatorial optimization, a methodological companion extending this paper's SA-RL synergy

related.title: Solving the Rubik's cube with deep reinforcement learning and search
related.relation: deep RL solving NP-hard combinatorial puzzle, parallel demonstration of RL generalization to complex discrete state spaces

figures.caption: Two classes of Hamiltonian problems: WSC model and nearest-neighbour spin glass, illustrating coupling topology and energy landscape structure
COOL policy network architecture: parallel convolutional layers plus LSTM module outputting temperature-change distribution and value function
RL policy performance on the WSC model: near-100% success across all initialization temperatures where classic SA fails at cold starts
Scaling of n99 with system size: RL outperforms linear SA at all lattice sizes with nearly two orders of magnitude advantage at L=14^2
Easy vs. difficult 10x10 spin glass instances: RL achieves 29% vs. 1% success on difficult cases with highly episode-specific policies
Learned value function V(s_t) on the 16x16 Ising model phase plane: agent assigns highest value to low-energy high-magnetization states nearest the global minimum
